---
title: "Fuzzy Match Villages"
format: html
---

Read in the data and the fuzzy matching library.

```{r}
library(readr)

# Read in the data
satp_df <- read_csv("data/satp_clean.csv")
shrug_df <- read_csv("data/shrid_loc_names.csv") 
```

Clean the data for use with `fuzzyjoin`.

```{r}
library(stringr)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

# convert village names to lower case in satp_df
satp_village_df <- satp_df |>
  mutate(across(state:constituency, str_to_lower)) |>
  rename_with(~ paste0(., "_name"), state:block) |>
  drop_na(village_name) 

# drop rows without village names in shrug_df

shrug_village_df <- shrug_df |>
  mutate(village_name = coalesce(village_name, town_name)) 
```

Try to fuzzy match the village names in satp_df with the village names in villages_df.

```{r}
# join_test <- satp_df |>
#   stringdist_left_join(villages_df, by = "village_name", max_dist = 2) |>
#   select(village_name.x, village_name.y, distance) #|>
#   #filter(distance < 2)
```

Does not work. Crashes because data frames are too big. 

Try looping through states and doing one state at a time.

```{r}
library(purrr)
library(fuzzyjoin)
library(dplyr)
library(stringdist)

# Create a list of unique states from satp_village_df
states <- unique(satp_village_df$state_name)

# Define a function to apply fuzzy join for each state
fuzzy_join_by_state <- function(state) {

  # Filter the data frames to only include the current state
  satp_state_subset <- satp_village_df |> filter(state_name == state)
  shrug_state_subset <- shrug_village_df |> filter(state_name == state)

  # Perform fuzzy join on both district and village names
  fuzzy_matches <- stringdist_left_join(satp_state_subset,
                                        shrug_state_subset,
                                        by = c("district_name", "village_name"),
                                        max_dist = .4,  
                                        method = "jw")

  # After joining, retain only the closest (best) match for each event 
  fuzzy_matches |>
    group_by(incident_number) |>
    slice_min(stringdist(district_name.x, district_name.y, method = "jw") + 
              stringdist(village_name.x, village_name.y, method = "jw"), 
              with_ties = FALSE) |>
    ungroup()
}

# Apply the fuzzy join function across all states
final_df <- map_dfr(states, fuzzy_join_by_state)
```

Clean up the data frame and inspect.

```{r}
  final_df <- final_df |>
    relocate(shrid2, .after = incident_number) |>
    relocate(state_name.y, .after = state_name.x) |>
    relocate(district_name.y, .after = district_name.x) |>
    relocate(subdistrict_name, .after = block_name) |>
    relocate(place_name, .after = other_areas) |>
    relocate(village_name.y, .after = village_name.x) 
```

Evaluate.

```{r}
# Calculate percent matched

final_df |>
  summarise(
    total_rows = n(),
    matched_rows = sum(!is.na(village_name.y)),
    percent_matched = matched_rows / total_rows * 100
  )
```
Jaro-Winkler (jw): 

Max distance of .2: 67.64% matched
Max distance of .3: 80.54% matched
Max distance of .4: 99.22% matched
No max distance: Does not work without crashing

Levenshtein (lv):

Max distance of 1: 43.46% matched
Max distance of 2: 57.17% matched
Max distance of 3: 65.19% matched
No max distance: 57.17% matched (so it seems like 2 is the default)

LV method does not seem to do as well at matching the districts.
 
As a next step, we could take a random sample of the matches and see what percentages are actually good. Then we could try it with an LLM. We could try BERT, T5, Gemma and Llama. I would start with Gemma because it is designed to handle linguistic variations across languages and diaclects.
